data.title: ADM, Ingredion slip after Trump says Coke will stop using corn syrup in U.S.
data.description:
Shares of corn syrup suppliers Archer Daniels Midland (NYSE:ADM) and Ingredion (NYSE:INGR) -1% and -1.1% in Thursday's trading, respectively, paring earlier declines of more than 6%, after President Trump said Coca-Cola (KO) would return to using cane sugar in its namesake soda.

Coca-Cola (KO) did not confirm the decision but said in a statement that "more details on new innovative offerings within our Coca-Cola product range will be shared soon."

Health and Human Services secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who has vowed to "Make America Healthy Again," has called high fructose corn syrup bad for Americans' health and "a formula for making you obese and diabetic."

The Corn Refiners Association trade group warned about potential job losses that could follow a Coca-Cola (KO) switch, arguing that "replacing high fructose corn syrup with cane sugar would cost thousands of American food manufacturing jobs, depress farm income, and boost imports of foreign sugar, all with no nutritional benefit."

U.S. sugar futures (SB1:COM) rose on the news, with the most-active October contract +1.1% in choppy trading.

data.title: British American Tobacco on track to break six-session rally
data.description:
British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) looked set to end a six-day winning streak as they moved into the red territory on Thursday. BTI lost 1.91% to trade at $ $51.27 during afternoon trading.

The stock has been rallying since July 9, when it ended 2.42% higher at $49.09 after Jefferies initiated coverage on the company with a Buy rating and named the stock its top pick in the tobacco sector.

Since then, BTI has gained 6.48% compared to a 0.01% rise in the broader markets. On a year-to-date basis, BTI has grown by over 12%, while in the last year it has advanced by more than 30%.

As per Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings, it is recommended to Hold the stock. It has a score of 3.31 out of 5, with an A+ grade for profitability and momentum but a D for growth. Wall Street analysts echoed the same sentiments and issued a Hold rating for BTI. However, Seeking Alpha analysts have a Buy call for the stock.

SA analyst The Asian Investor has assigned a Strong Buy rating to the stock, describing it as ‘a compelling value investment’, citing strong capital returns fueled by robust free cash flow and a low earnings-based valuation.

The analyst also argued that BTI’s growing momentum in alternative products like Velo is helping offset declines in traditional tobacco, thereby supporting future growth in a challenging market.

“Aggressive marketing and ambitious medium-term goals in smoke-free products position BTI for sustained top line and earnings momentum,” they added.

Sensor Unlimited also reiterated its Buy rating for the stock following the company’s trade update released in early June.

“After examining the updates, I keep seeing a biased reward/risk curve despite the large price rally since my last writing. The leading catalysts in my view are the progress its New Category products have reported and its capital allocation choices,” the analyst noted.

data.title: Cypto thefts hit $2.17B so far in 2025, exceeding all 2024 losses: Chainalysis
data.description:
More than $2.17B has been stolen from crypto services and individual wallets so far this year, already surpassing 2024's total losses, according to a report published Thursday.

Stolen crypto funds are projected to potentially hit $4B by the end of 2025, underscoring how "the stakes for cybersecurity have never been higher." blockchain intelligence company Chainalysis said.

Notably, crypto exchange Bybit's $1.5B hack made up most (69%) of the total service losses YTD as of June.

In Q1, hackers stole $1.67B, a 303% surge from the previous quarter, blockchain security firm CeriK had noted. The Bybit attack occurred in Q1.

"At $1.5 billion, this single incident not only represents the largest crypto theft in history, but also accounts for approximately 69% of all funds stolen from services this year," Chainalysis said.

More recently, it added, theft from personal wallets is becoming a larger slice of total crypto losses, with attacks on individual users accounting for about 23% of all stolen funds this year.

data.title: Citizens outlines 3% to 4% Q3 net interest income growth as new strategic initiatives accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Citizens Financial Group (CFG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Bruce Winfield Van Saun, CEO, highlighted "strong financial results today that exceeded expectations, notwithstanding tremendous uncertainty in the macro environment during the quarter." He noted strong net interest income (NII) growth of 3.3% sequentially, net loan growth across Consumer, Private Bank, and Commercial, 10% fee growth, and flat expenses resulting in 500 basis points of operating leverage. Van Saun emphasized the company's "rock solid" balance sheet and continued share repurchases. He also announced a new multiyear initiative titled "Reimagining the bank," led by Brendan Coughlin and top leaders, to redesign service delivery using Gen AI and Agentic AI, involving changes to the organizational model, technology, and skill development.

  • John F. Woods, CFO, stated, "we delivered EPS of $0.92 for the second quarter, a $0.15 or 19% improvement over Q1. Net interest income for the quarter was up 3.3%, driven by margin expansion and interest-earning asset growth." Woods further noted record wealth and card fees, modest growth in capital markets, and well-managed expenses. He added, "we ended the quarter with CET1 at 10.6%, while also executing $200 million in stock buybacks during the quarter." Woods also highlighted that the Private Bank contributed $0.06 to EPS this quarter, up from $0.04 previously, with loan growth of $1.2 billion.

Outlook

  • Management guided for Q3 net interest income to be up approximately 3% to 4%, with net interest margin expected to improve by approximately 5 basis points and interest-earning assets up slightly. Noninterest income is projected to increase in the low single digits, led by rebounding capital markets. Expenses are expected to rise approximately 1% to 1.5% due to continued Private Bank build-out and strong fee revenues. Credit trends are projected to improve modestly, and CET1 is anticipated to remain stable, including share repurchases of about $75 million. The full-year outlook remains broadly in line with earlier guidance, with a clear path to a 16% to 18% ROTCE target.

Financial Results

  • Citizens reported EPS of $0.92 for the second quarter, up $0.15 from Q1. Net interest income rose 3.3% sequentially, aided by a 5 basis points net interest margin expansion to 2.95%. Fee income increased 10% from the previous quarter, with record performances in wealth and card fees. Expenses were broadly stable, driving about 5% positive operating leverage and improving the efficiency ratio to below 65%. The Private Bank delivered its strongest loan growth to date, adding $1.2 billion to reach $4.9 billion in period-end loans. Average deposits were up 1%, with a favorable shift toward noninterest-bearing accounts. Net charge-offs were 48 basis points, down from 51 basis points in Q1 (adjusted for a noncore education loan sale), and nonaccrual loans declined 4% sequentially. The allowance for credit losses declined slightly to 1.59%. CET1 ratio remained at 10.6%, with $200 million in share repurchases and a total of $385 million returned to shareholders including dividends.

Q&A

  • Ryan Matthew Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the sources of loan growth and borrower sentiment. CEO Van Saun responded that "it felt a little like an inflection point that we finally saw all 3 of the businesses, commercial, consumer, private bank have net loan growth," and expects growth to continue, with tailwinds from possible rate declines. Brendan Coughlin provided figures for retail loan growth and highlighted Citizens' leading position in HELOC originations, while Donald H. McCree noted business optimism and pipeline strength, especially in new markets such as Florida and California.

  • Erika Najarian, UBS: Asked about deposit growth and funding the outlook. CFO Woods indicated strong momentum in low-cost deposit trends and seasonal factors supporting deposit growth. Coughlin added that retail deposits outperformed peer averages, and McCree mentioned new wins in payments and embedded finance, contributing to deposit mix improvements.

  • Matthew Derek O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: Requested details on the "Reimagining the bank" initiative. CEO Van Saun described a multiyear transformation to leverage AI and improve efficiency. Coughlin added that "everything is on the table," including technology, real estate, vendor simplification, and organizational structure, with a goal to self-fund investments through quick wins and longer-term initiatives.

  • Kenneth Michael Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous: Inquired about capital markets pipeline and fee outlook. CEO Van Saun described a "huge tailwind" from deals delayed to Q3 and growing deal pipelines, while McCree noted optimism about new money and refinancing activities in the private credit complex.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed optimism about loan growth and fee momentum but were probing on deposit growth sustainability, margin outlook, and competitive pressures. The tone was slightly positive, with questions reflecting confidence but also seeking clarity on execution and risks.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. CEO Van Saun and CFO Woods consistently emphasized strong performance, forward momentum, and cautious optimism, using phrases such as "we feel well positioned" and "feeling pretty good about that range." The tone was more upbeat than in the previous quarter, reflecting improved results and outlook.

  • Compared to the prior quarter, both analysts and management showed increased confidence, with less focus on macro uncertainties and more discussion of strategic initiatives and growth opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language strengthened, with management projecting higher NII growth and more positive operating leverage for Q3. Strategic focus shifted toward executing the "Reimagining the bank" initiative and accelerating Private Bank growth. Analysts focused more on growth execution and competitive positioning, rather than macro headwinds. Key metrics such as EPS, NII, and loan growth improved quarter over quarter, and management's tone was more constructive and future-focused. Analysts' tone shifted from cautious in Q1 to seeking details on growth drivers and new initiatives in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and their potential impact on deal flow. However, they noted that "the worst-case outcomes on tariffs seem to be behind us" while acknowledging the need to monitor developments. Credit quality risks were discussed, particularly in commercial real estate and general office segments, but management indicated that "peak credit losses are behind us." Analysts raised questions about deposit cost trends, competitive pressures on loan pricing, and the pace of Private Bank deposit growth. Management addressed these with confidence, outlining strategic responses and continued discipline in capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

Citizens Financial Group management emphasized strong second-quarter results, driven by margin expansion, loan and fee growth, and disciplined expenses. The company reaffirmed its positive outlook for the remainder of 2025, projecting further net interest income gains and credit improvement. Strategic initiatives, including the large-scale adoption of AI and digital transformation, are expected to enhance efficiency and support long-term growth, while the Private Bank continues to outperform targets. Management remains focused on execution, prudent capital management, and leveraging emerging opportunities to build a distinctive, high-performing bank.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Petrobras eyes return to retail fuel sales, board set to discuss - Bloomberg
data.description:
Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) is considering a return to retail fuel sales after Brazil President Lula da Silva and the company's CEO complained about high pump prices, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

Four years after exiting the business now known as Vibra Energia, Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) board will meet this week to discuss amending the company's strategic plan to include a presence in the retail sector, according to the report.

It is not clear if such a move would involve trying to fully re-nationalize Vibra or buying a stake in the convenience store operator and distributor of cooking fuels and other petroleum products.

Lula has often complained that wholesale price cuts by Petrobras (PBR) for gasoline, diesel and other products have not flowed through to consumers at the retail level.

Separately, Petrobras (PBR) may redirect the oil it sells to the U.S., sending more to Asia and Pacific markets due to higher tariffs the U.S. announced on Brazil, CEO Magda Chambriard told Reuters on Thursday, adding the U.S. is not an essential market for the company.

"It is not much that we export [to the U.S.]. In general, we are not very worried," the CEO said in her first public comments about the 50% tariff announced last week by President Trump.

Exports to the U.S. represented 4% of Petrobras' (PBR) total oil shipments in Q1, and Brazil has supplied less than 3% of the oil consumed by the U.S. so far in 2025, according to the StoneX consultancy.

data.title: CVS Health shares fall for seven consecutive sessions
data.description:
CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) shares extended six straight sessions of losses, as the stock slipped 0.9% at $63.26 on Thursday.

The healthcare company fell 4.3% in the preceding six sessions. The stock has gained nearly 42% so far this year, compared to a 6.9% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

CVS is down 6% over the past one month.

Recently, a federal judge ordered CVS Health's Omnicare unit to pay nearly $949M as part of a whistleblower lawsuit that accused the pharmacy of charging the federal government improperly for prescription drug claims.

 
 

"Despite a short-term recovery in 2025, CVS faces ongoing challenges: declining retail, regulatory threats to PBM, and uncertain Medicare Advantage profitability," pointed out a Seeking Alpha analysis.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, CVS has a Hold rating with a score of 3.5 out of 5. The company received A+ in the prospect of profitability, B- in momentum, while it got D- growth factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 21 analysts gave CVS a Buy and above rating. Seven analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, while none recommended Sell or lower.

 
 
 
 

Seeking Alpha analysts are cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

data.title: Ambac reports Wisconsin commissioner slates hearing on legacy business sale
data.description:
Ambac Financial (NYSE:AMBC) stock surged 14% in Thursday afternoon trading after the Wisconsin Office of the Commissioner of Insurance scheduled a hearing on the sale of Ambac Assurance Corp., one of the final steps in shedding the legacy business, the company said on Thursday.

The hearing is set for Sep. 3, 2025 at 9:00 AM CT.  A pre-hearing conference related to the transaction is scheduled for Aug. 4 at 3:00 PM CT.

“We are pleased that a hearing date has been set, one of the final steps in the Wisconsin regulatory approval process,” Ambac (NYSE:AMBC) President and CEO Claude LeBlanc said. “We are eager to complete the Sale and cement our transformation into a pure-play specialty P&C insurance platform.”

data.title: Abbott signals high single-digit sales growth outlook, eyes biosimilars launch and pipeline momentum amid $1B diagnostics headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Abbott Laboratories (ABT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert B. Ford stated, "At the halfway point of the year, we are on track with our key priorities and objectives. In the first half, we delivered high single-digit sales growth over 100 basis points of margin expansion in both gross margin and operating margin, double-digit earnings per share, and we achieved a number of important milestones related to advancing key programs in our new product pipeline." Ford highlighted 7.5% sales growth in Q2 excluding COVID testing, and adjusted earnings per share of $1.26, reflecting 11% growth versus the prior year and 16% sequential growth versus Q1.
  • Ford noted strong Adult Nutrition growth at 6.5%, robust performance in the EPD business with nearly 8% sales growth, and 12% Medical Devices growth driven by diabetes care, heart failure, structural heart, electrophysiology, and cardiac rhythm management. Diabetes Care sales of continuous glucose monitors reached $1.9 billion, up 19.5%, supported by international market expansion and product innovation, including a first-of-its-kind collaboration with Epic for Libre sensor integration.
  • Ford reported headwinds in Diagnostics, with sales down 1.5% due to COVID testing declines and China volume-based procurement, representing a projected $700 million headwind for full year 2025. Excluding China, Core Lab Diagnostics grew 8%.
  • Ford emphasized the launch of the Tendyne mitral replacement valve following FDA approval, expansion in the mitral valve replacement market, and the development of a new transfemoral mitral valve replacement product with FDA breakthrough designation. He also highlighted strong uptake of AVEIR leadless pacemaker, double-digit growth in cardiac rhythm management, and 14% heart failure business growth.
  • CFO Philip P. Boudreau stated, "Sales increased 6.9% or 7.5% when excluding the COVID testing-related sales. Adjusted earnings per share of $1.26 increased 11% compared to the prior year and was above the consensus estimate." Boudreau reported adjusted gross margin at 57% of sales, up 100 basis points, and adjusted operating margin at 22.9%. He forecast Q3 adjusted EPS in the range of $1.28 to $1.32 and anticipated a relatively neutral FX impact for the full year with a favorable 2% impact on Q3 sales.

Outlook

  • Boudreau guided for Q3 adjusted EPS of $1.28 to $1.32. Management reaffirmed full-year expectations of high single-digit growth and double-digit EPS growth, despite headwinds from diagnostics and tariffs. Ford explained, "Even with that $1 billion, we're still forecasting high single-digit growth and absorbing the impact of tariffs, which we now expect to be just under $200 million of impact." He projected that the current diagnostics headwinds, including China and COVID testing declines, will be lapped next year, with new product launches and biosimilars rollout expected to drive momentum in 2026.

Financial Results

  • Abbott reported Q2 sales growth of 6.9%, or 7.5% excluding COVID testing sales. Adjusted EPS reached $1.26, marking 11% growth year-over-year and 16% sequential growth. Adjusted gross margin improved to 57% of sales, and adjusted operating margin to 22.9%. Adult Nutrition sales rose 3.5%, EPD sales increased nearly 8%, and Medical Devices grew 12% in Q2. Diabetes Care sales of continuous glucose monitors climbed to $1.9B, growing 19.5%.
  • Diagnostic segment sales declined 1.5%, mainly due to COVID testing and China market pressures. Foreign exchange provided a 0.5% favorable impact on sales.

Q&A

  • David Harrison Roman, Goldman Sachs, asked about the transience of diagnostics headwinds, with Ford responding, "Together with the funding for HIV testing, that's over $1 billion of headwind. And even with that $1 billion, we're still forecasting high single-digit growth... So yes, it will be good to see these elements... behind us next year."
  • Robert Justin Marcus, JPMorgan, inquired about trends in diabetes and electrophysiology, to which Ford detailed, "Another great quarter of Libre, almost 26% in the U.S., an acceleration internationally... The new sensor you referenced, I think, is going to definitely accelerate there." He described the Dual-analyte sensor as a significant change for intensive users.
  • Lawrence H. Biegelsen, Wells Fargo, asked about competitive bidding for CGM and the ketone sensor. Ford explained, "If CMS chooses to do competitive bidding... it's really going to be the DMEs that are going to be the ones doing the bidding, not the CGM manufacturers... I don't expect there to be an impact on Abbott." On the ketone sensor: "Do we think that, that can accelerate our share gain? 100%, yes."
  • Vijay Muniyappa Kumar, Evercore ISI, asked about CRM business metrics and EPS guidance offsets. Ford stated, "I think it's fundamentally changed the growth trajectory of our CRM business... achieving this is very sustainable." Boudreau quantified tariff impact as "a little less than $200 million impact here, so down from previous estimates."
  • Travis Lee Steed, BofA Securities, and Danielle Joy Antalffy, UBS, probed on M&A opportunities and structural heart TAM expansion. Ford said, "It's a good environment for M&A, good opportunities out there, got a strong organic pipeline. So that allows us to be a lot more selective here."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, focusing on future growth drivers, the impact of headwinds, and product innovation. Analysts asked probing questions about sustainability and recovery, reflecting some caution but optimism regarding pipeline and market share.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, acknowledging headwinds but emphasizing resilience and sustainable growth. Ford used phrases such as "we're still forecasting high single-digit growth" and "we're very bullish on this segment," signaling confidence in long-term prospects.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady and optimistic, with a more detailed breakdown of strategies to address diagnostics and tariff headwinds, and a greater emphasis on upcoming product launches and biosimilars.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remains consistent, with management reaffirming high single-digit growth and double-digit EPS targets, while specifying the magnitude and expected duration of diagnostics and tariff headwinds.
  • Strategic focus this quarter shifted toward pipeline execution, biosimilars rollout, and mitigating diagnostics headwinds, compared to prior quarter’s emphasis on initial tariff response and margin improvement.
  • Analysts’ focus evolved from tariff mitigation and manufacturing flexibility in Q1 to product pipeline momentum and market share acceleration in Q2.
  • Key metrics such as sales growth, adjusted EPS, and margin expansion were highlighted in both quarters, but this quarter included more concrete details on product launches, biosimilars, and international expansion.
  • Management’s confidence level remained high, with more explicit discussion of overcoming current headwinds and leveraging pipeline strength for 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited diagnostics headwinds from COVID testing declines, China volume-based procurement, and U.S. foreign aid funding for HIV testing, collectively representing over $1 billion in headwinds for 2025.
  • Tariffs are expected to impact results by just under $200 million, with ongoing efforts to mitigate effects through global manufacturing network adjustments.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of CRM growth, competitive bidding impacts on CGM, and EPS flexibility in the face of tariffs and FX volatility. Management outlined ongoing mitigation strategies and long-term resilience plans.

Final Takeaway

Abbott Laboratories’ Q2 2025 call underscored the resilience of its diversified business model, delivering high single-digit organic sales growth, double-digit EPS expansion, and significant margin improvement despite notable headwinds in diagnostics and tariffs. Management reaffirmed its outlook for the remainder of 2025 and highlighted the momentum of its product pipeline—including biosimilars launches, device innovations, and international expansion—as key drivers for sustainable growth through 2026 and beyond, positioning the company to absorb current challenges and capitalize on future opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cintas outlines $11B–$11.15B fiscal 2026 revenue target while advancing margin expansion and M&A activity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cintas Corporation (CTAS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Todd M. Schneider, CEO, opened the call by highlighting a strong fourth quarter and full year, stating that "total revenue grew 8% to $2.67 billion" in Q4, with an organic growth rate of 9%. Schneider noted organic growth of 7.2% in Uniform Rental and Facility Services and 18.5% in the First Aid and Safety segment, adding that "All Other, which includes our Fire Protection Services and Uniform Direct Sales grew organically by 11.1%." He emphasized the company's technology and infrastructure investments, including SAP and SmartTruck, and described capital allocation as a key pillar. For fiscal 2025, revenue was reported at $10.34 billion, an increase of 7.7%, with operating margins at an all-time high of 22.8%. Schneider announced, "We expect our revenue to be in the range of $11 billion to $11.15 billion, a total growth rate of 6.4% to 7.8%. We expect diluted EPS to be in the range of $4.71 to $4.85, a growth rate of 7% to 10.2%."
  • James N. Rozakis, COO, noted that "our retention rates are right at our all-time highs and pricing continued to be at our historical levels." Rozakis detailed segment mix and margin improvements, emphasizing the contribution of supply chain and efficiency initiatives. He provided an illustrative customer win where Cintas expanded a facility services relationship into uniform rentals and culinary department solutions, underscoring cross-selling opportunities.
  • Scott A. Garula, CFO, stated "we generated $1.6 billion of free cash flow" in fiscal 2025 and highlighted $232.9 million in acquisitions, the largest M&A year in nearly two decades (excluding 2017's G&K deal). He said, "we returned over $1.5 billion to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks," and explained that guidance for fiscal 2026 assumes "no future acquisitions," a constant FX rate, and no significant economic disruptions.

Outlook

  • Cintas projects fiscal 2026 revenue of $11 billion to $11.15 billion and diluted EPS of $4.71 to $4.85. Management expects a revenue growth rate of 6.4% to 7.8% and EPS growth of 7% to 10.2%. Assumptions include no future acquisitions, a constant FX rate, a fiscal 2026 effective tax rate of 20%, and no significant buybacks or economic disruptions. Schneider stated, "Our results reflect the strength of our strategy, and the value we provide in helping our customers meet their image, safety, cleanliness and compliance needs."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue reached $2.67 billion, up 8%. Gross margin for the quarter grew 9.1% year-over-year from 49.2% to 49.7%. Operating income as a percentage of revenue increased 9.1%, and diluted EPS rose 9% to $1.09. For the full year, diluted EPS was $4.40, a 16.1% increase over the prior year. Fiscal 2025 operating margins reached 22.8%, up from 21.6%. Free cash flow was $1.6 billion. Capital expenditures were $408.9 million (4% of revenue), and M&A spend was $232.9 million. Dividend payments totaled almost $612 million, marking 41 consecutive annual increases. Share repurchases were approximately $935 million.
  • Segment highlights include Q4 organic growth of 7.2% in Uniform Rental and Facility Services, 18.5% in First Aid and Safety, and 12.1% in Fire Protection Services. Gross margin percentages by business: Uniform Rental and Facility Services 49%, First Aid and Safety 56.8%, Fire Protection Services 49.3%, Uniform Direct Sale 41.6%.

Q&A

  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs, asked about the selling environment and client sentiment. Schneider replied, "No real change to the customer behavior, sales cycles. New business remains strong. Our retention rates are still at very attractive levels."
  • Tong followed up on the narrowing rate of margin expansion. Schneider explained, "last year, Q4 was by far our best profit quarter. So the comparables were certainly tougher."
  • Jasper Bibb, Truist Securities, queried about the cadence of the 2026 outlook and incremental margins. Schneider stated, "the guide calls for EPS growth of 7% to 10.2%, which infers margin expansion throughout the guide." On ad stops, he noted, "we have some customers that are absolutely thriving… the net-net is our customer base is still performing well."
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays, probed the drivers of incremental margins. Schneider responded, "we will be continuing to invest in SAP in our fire business... that's all contemplated in our guide."
  • Timothy Mulrooney, William Blair, highlighted Q4 organic growth above expectations. Schneider attributed this to "more discrete type onetime benefits in the area of our First Aid business" and a strong close in Uniform Direct Sale.
  • Other topics covered include cost trajectory amid tariffs, M&A pipeline, pricing strategy, verticals, First Aid business momentum, and CapEx planning.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on margin sustainability, cost pressures, pricing power, segment growth, and M&A strategy. The overall tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions seeking clarity on guidance assumptions and segment drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, with Schneider frequently expressing that "we like the spot we're in" and "we think we're well positioned." The tone remained consistent with the prior quarter, focused on discipline and opportunity despite macro uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management continued to stress execution and discipline, with a similar level of confidence and transparency. Analyst tone remained steady, with questions focused on guidance detail and segment momentum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 is more robust and explicit, with a revenue range of $11 billion to $11.15 billion and EPS of $4.71 to $4.85, compared to prior guidance for fiscal 2025. There is no material change in pricing strategy, still at historic levels. Margin expansion remains a focus, with continued investments in technology, SAP, and SmartTruck initiatives.
  • The scale of M&A activity was higher in Q4, with $232.9 million deployed, the most in nearly 20 years. Management appointments changed, with Scott Garula now CFO. Analysts continue to focus on margin drivers and the sustainability of growth in First Aid and Fire Protection segments.
  • Management's sentiment and confidence levels remained consistent, with ongoing emphasis on capital allocation, efficiency, and operational leverage.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty, tariff and trade policy changes, and input cost challenges as potential risks. Schneider noted, "there are certain -- or excuse me, clearly there's more uncertainty in the marketplace with tariff trade tax -- or excuse me, tariff/trade taxes."
  • Supply chain disruptions, cost pressures from tariffs, and the ongoing SAP implementation in Fire Protection were acknowledged as factors being actively managed and reflected in guidance.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the sustainability of above-average margin performance, the impact of onetime benefits in Q4, and the ability to maintain momentum in First Aid and Uniform Direct Sale.

Final Takeaway

Cintas delivered strong Q4 and full-year results, driven by double-digit growth in core segments and robust margin improvement. With a clear revenue target of $11 billion to $11.15 billion and EPS of $4.71 to $4.85 set for fiscal 2026, management emphasized ongoing investments in technology, capital deployment, and operational excellence to support continued growth, while acknowledging macro uncertainty and input cost risks. The company maintains high retention rates, disciplined capital allocation, and a strong M&A pipeline, positioning itself to navigate evolving market conditions and deliver sustained value to shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ge Aerospace raises 2028 profit outlook to $11.5B with margin expansion above 21%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: General Electric Company (GE) (GE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • H. Lawrence Culp, Chairman & CEO, highlighted GE Aerospace's strong positioning as a global leader in propulsion, services, and systems, noting "our Commercial Engines & Services business, or CES, is servicing and growing the industry's most extensive commercial installed base," and described robust services demand, a $175 billion backlog, and focus on safety driven by their proprietary FLIGHT DECK operating model.
  • Culp announced significant strategic wins, including "Qatar Airways announced a significant expansion of their fleet with a deal for over 400 GEnx and GE9X engines, marking the largest wide-body win in GE Aerospace history," and a $5 billion U.S. Air Force contract for F110 engines.
  • Culp outlined investments, stating "last year, we announced we'll invest more than $1 billion in our MRO and component repair facilities over the next 5 years," and discussed an expanded MRO network and operational enhancements with AI-enabled inspection tools.
  • Rahul Ghai, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "we had a strong second quarter, with improvement across all key metrics. Orders were up 27%. Revenue was over $10 billion, up 23% with CES growing 30% and DPT, up 7%. Profit was $2.3 billion, up over $400 million or 23%, driven by services volume and price, which supported margins reaching 23%."

Outlook

  • GE Aerospace raised its 2028 outlook, with Culp stating, "For 2028, we're raising our outlook for profit and free cash flow by $1.5 billion versus our prior view driven by strong operating and commercial services performance. We expect to sustain strong adjusted revenue growth at a double-digit compounded rate...adjusted EPS reaching roughly $8.40. Operating profit is expected to reach approximately $11.5 billion with margins expanding to more than 21%."
  • Ghai added, "We now expect total revenue growth of mid-teens, up from low double digits. We are increasing revenue growth expectations for commercial services to high teens and commercial equipment to high teens to 20%. This supports total CES revenue growth of high teens. Our DPT expectations are unchanged from mid- to high single-digit revenue growth."
  • Free cash flow guidance was raised to a range of $6.5 billion to $6.9 billion for 2025 and at least $8.5 billion by 2028.

Financial Results

  • Orders increased by 27%, revenue exceeded $10 billion (up 23%), and profit reached $2.3 billion (up 23%) for the quarter, with EPS at $1.66 (up 38%). Free cash flow was $2.1 billion, nearly doubling year-over-year.
  • CES segment delivered 30% revenue growth and a 33% profit increase; margins expanded to 27.9%.
  • DPT segment posted 7% revenue growth and profit of approximately $360 million, with margins at 14.1%.
  • CES services revenue rose 29%, supported by strong spare parts and shop visit growth. Total engine deliveries increased 45% (commercial up 37%, defense up 84%).
  • Capital allocation plans now target $24 billion of cash returned to shareholders through 2026, up from the prior $19 billion plan.

Q&A

  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: Questioned the implied second half EBIT decline versus historical seasonality. Ghai explained, "we expected a strong start to the year this year...corporate expenses also typically step up in the second half of the year...and then there's an R&D step up...we are maintaining some conservatismness around departures."
  • Myles Walton, Wolfe Research: Asked about pricing assumptions to 2028 and retirements. Culp responded, "as we look forward, we're probably looking at something on a mid-single-digit basis at a gross level low single digit on a net basis, at least with respect to spare parts," and Ghai said, "as the fleet ages, this should pick up...that gets us to our ratio of 3% to 4%, somewhere in that zone."
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Asked about LEAP aftermarket profitability. Ghai noted, "we are seeing good progress on LEAP service profitability in '25. And I think that will continue to improve as we get into the outer years, profit equals CFM56 margins, profit equals CFM56 [dollars] by 2030."
  • Gavin Parsons, UBS: Inquired about conservatism in second-half guidance and customer fleet planning. Culp stated, "I don't think we've seen any change in customer behavior...those backlogs are real."
  • Ron Epstein, Bank of America: Asked about confidence in RISE technology. Culp cited "over 350 program tests at the module subsystem level" and confidence in the open fan architecture.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently pressed management on conservatism in guidance, supply chain improvements, and durability upgrades, with a neutral to slightly skeptical tone, especially around EBIT seasonality and pricing longevity.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we're well positioned," "we feel better about the year than we did back in April," and "we are encouraged with the outlook."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from caution regarding macro uncertainty and tariffs to increased confidence, as reflected in the raised guidance and repeated references to backlog strength.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a significant upward revision in revenue growth guidance (mid-teens from low double digits), profit ($8.2-$8.5 billion from $7.8-$8.2 billion), and free cash flow ($6.5-$6.9 billion from $6.3-$6.8 billion).
  • Management emphasized stronger supply chain execution and operational momentum versus last quarter's concerns around supply constraints and tariffs.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on margin cadence, supply chain, and pricing, but Q2's Q&A reflected heightened interest in the sustainability of growth and the durability of profitability improvements.
  • Strategic focus shifted from managing headwinds to capitalizing on operational and commercial wins, with increased capital return targets.
  • Management confidence was higher, with repeated mentions of "momentum" and a more constructive outlook on both commercial and defense segments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing supply chain constraints, stating "the material delivery environment is expected to remain tight...we are really not expecting any let off in the installation that we have been seeing."
  • Tariffs remain a potential risk, with a $500 million impact in 2025 still expected but being offset through cost controls and pricing.
  • R&D and corporate expenses are expected to step up in the second half, impacting EBIT seasonality.
  • No major shifts in customer fleet planning or order cancellations were reported, but management continues to monitor macroeconomic and geopolitical factors closely.

Final Takeaway

GE Aerospace delivered a robust Q2, increasing its 2025 and 2028 profit and cash flow outlooks amid strong operational and commercial momentum. Management emphasized continued investment in technology, supply chain, and shareholder returns, targeting double-digit annual revenue growth, $11.5 billion profit, and more than 21% margins by 2028. The updated guidance reflects confidence in the durability of growth, robust backlog, and supply chain execution, while ongoing risks from tariffs and supply constraints remain under close watch.
